skill.name: agent-skill-trust-check
skill.description: Static pre-install trust review for SKILL.md, OpenClaw, Hermes, MCP, and agent-skill marketplace packages before they request local, account, payment, or external access.
skill.summary: Pre-install security review for AI agent skills and marketplace packages.
skill.author: TateLyman

security_audit.summary: All 15 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES. This skill is a README/documentation file for a static security review tool. Backtick code blocks show example bash commands for running the tool. URLs are documentation references to the source repository and a paid marketplace listing. Keywords like 'private keys' and 'secrets' appear in the context of describing what the tool checks for in OTHER skills. No prompt injection or malicious intent detected. Final risk level: LOW.

low_findings.title: Shell backtick execution in code blocks (FALSE POSITIVE - DISMISSED)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged markdown code blocks as shell execution. These are example bash commands showing how to run the review tool (git clone, npm run, node, npx). They are documentation in a README file, not active execution code.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in documentation (FALSE POSITIVE - DISMISSED)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged two URLs as network access. Line 35 shows a git clone URL for the tool's source repository. Line 70 links to a paid marketplace listing. Both are documentation references, not actual network calls from the skill.

low_findings.title: Crypto/private key keyword mention (FALSE POSITIVE - DISMISSED)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged line 58 for mentioning 'private keys' and 'secrets'. This line describes what the TOOL checks for in OTHER skills during review. It is documentation of the tool's detection capabilities, not an actual credential access.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm reference (FALSE POSITIVE - DISMISSED)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged lines 3, 18, and 19 for weak crypto. None of these lines reference any cryptographic algorithm. Line 3 is the YAML description header. Lines 18-19 list review categories for the tool. This appears to be a false match on unrelated terminology.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance reference (FALSE POSITIVE - DISMISSED)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged line 68 as reconnaissance. Line 68 contains a reference to a paid marketplace listing (orkai.ai). This is a documentation link, not reconnaissance activity.

low_findings.title: Dangerous combination heuristic (FALSE POSITIVE - DISMISSED)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged a heuristic combination of code execution, network, and credential access patterns. All underlying patterns are false positives (documentation examples and descriptions). No actual obfuscation or malicious behavior detected.

content.user_title: Review agent skills for security risks before install
content.value_statement: Installing untrusted agent skills can expose your system to shell injection, data exfiltration, and credential theft. This skill performs a static pre-install review of SKILL.md files to flag dangerous patterns before you grant access.
actual_capabilities: Scans SKILL.md files for shell execution and destructive command patterns
Flags credential access, secret reading, and wallet/payment action patterns
Separates basic provenance signals from risky behavioral signals
Produces a prioritized patch order for maintainers to resolve
Reviews skills from OpenClaw, Hermes, MCP, and Claude Code directories
Checks for prompt-boundary issues and instruction override attempts
limitations: Performs static analysis only, does not execute or sandbox the target skill
May produce false positives for legitimate patterns that look like threats
Cannot detect obfuscated, encoded, or dynamically constructed malicious code
Does not verify runtime behavior, network destinations, or post-install actions

use_cases.title: Vet community skills before installation
use_cases.description: A developer finds a promising skill on a community marketplace. Before installing, they run this review to check for shell commands, credential access, and network calls in the SKILL.md file.

use_cases.title: Add trust screening to CI/CD pipeline
use_cases.description: A platform team integrates this review into their CI workflow. Every new skill submission is automatically scanned and flagged if it contains dangerous patterns, ensuring only vetted skills reach users.

use_cases.title: Audit existing installed skills
use_cases.description: A security-conscious user runs this review against all currently installed skills to identify any that request shell access, read environment variables, or send data to remote endpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Quick trust check for a new skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the SKILL.md file at [path] for security risks before I install it. Flag any shell commands, network calls, credential reads, or instruction overrides. Give me a pass or fail recommendation.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user wants a fast yes-or-no recommendation before installing a skill.

prompt_templates.title: Deep review with risk report
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a thorough security review of [path]/SKILL.md. For each risky pattern found, explain why it is dangerous and suggest a fix. Group findings into: shell execution, network access, credential handling, persistence, and prompt injection. Rate the overall risk as low, medium, or high.
prompt_templates.scenario: A developer needs a detailed risk report with remediation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Compare two skills for trustworthiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review both [path-a]/SKILL.md and [path-b]/SKILL.md for security risks. Compare the two side by side. Tell me which one has fewer dangerous patterns and explain the key differences in their risk profiles.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user deciding between two similar skills from different authors.

prompt_templates.title: CI-friendly JSON output for automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review [path]/SKILL.md and output the results as structured data. Include: number of findings, their severity levels, file locations, and a pass/fail verdict. Format the output so it can be consumed by an automated pipeline.
prompt_templates.scenario: A platform engineer integrating trust checks into an automated deployment pipeline.

output_examples.input: Check if this marketplace skill is safe to install. It claims to format code but mentions executing shell commands.
output: The skill requests shell access through backtick execution patterns. It also references environment variables that may contain API keys.
Recommendation: Review with caution. The shell access pattern is high risk. Consider asking the author to remove command execution before approving.

output_examples.input: I found a skill that connects to an external service. Is that a problem?
output: Network access alone is not automatically dangerous. The context matters: is the URL hardcoded and known, or user-configurable?
This skill connects to a documented API endpoint. The risk is medium. Verify the destination is trusted and data sent is non-sensitive.

best_practices: Always manually review flagged items before making a trust decision. Static analysis finds patterns, not intent.
Combine this static review with runtime observation when possible. Some threats only appear during execution.
Keep the review criteria up to date as new attack patterns emerge in the agent skill ecosystem.
anti_patterns: Skipping flagged items without manual review because they seem common or benign. Familiarity breeds complacency.
Assuming the absence of flags means a skill is completely safe. Static analysis cannot detect all threat types.
Using the review only once at install time and never re-checking. Skills can change between versions.

faq.question: What types of risks does this skill detect?
faq.answer: It detects shell command execution, destructive commands, secrets and credential access, wallet and payment actions, network output, persistence mechanisms, and prompt-boundary or instruction override attempts.

faq.question: Can this skill guarantee a skill is safe?
faq.answer: No. Static analysis cannot detect all threats. Some malicious code is obfuscated, encoded, or only activates at runtime. Use this as one layer of a broader trust strategy.

faq.question: Does this skill execute the target skill?
faq.answer: No. This is a static pre-install check. It reads the SKILL.md file and analyzes the text without running any code from the target skill.

faq.question: What is a false positive and how do I handle one?
faq.answer: A false positive is a pattern that looks dangerous but is actually safe. For example, a skill that documents example commands in a README. Review each flag manually and dismiss it if you understand and accept the context.

faq.question: Does this work with all skill marketplace formats?
faq.answer: It works with SKILL.md files from OpenClaw, Hermes, MCP-linked bundles, Claude Code directories, and standard GitHub repository skill packages.

faq.question: Is this skill itself safe to use?
faq.answer: Yes. This skill only reads and analyzes text files. It does not execute commands, make network calls, or access credentials. It was reviewed and no security issues were found.
